questions.optionB: Semi-conservative (each new double helix has one old and one new strand)
questions.explanation: Semi-conservative replication (proved by Meselson-Stahl experiment using N15/N14 CsCl density gradient centrifugation): each parental strand serves as a template for a new complementary strand → each daughter DNA has one old strand + one new strand. DNA replication: bidirectional from origins of replication. Leading strand: continuous (5'→3'). Lagging strand: discontinuous (Okazaki fragments, each ~100-200 nt in eukaryotes, ~1000-2000 in prokaryotes, joined by DNA ligase). Reference: Harper's — DNA Replication.

questions.questionText: The enzyme that synthesizes RNA from a DNA template during transcription is:
questions.explanation: RNA polymerase: transcribes DNA → RNA (5'→3' direction, reads template 3'→5'). Does NOT require a primer (unlike DNA polymerase). Eukaryotic RNA polymerases: RNA Pol I (rRNA — 28S, 18S, 5.8S), RNA Pol II (mRNA, snRNA — inhibited by α-amanitin at low dose), RNA Pol III (tRNA, 5S rRNA, snRNAs). Prokaryotes: single RNA polymerase (core enzyme + σ factor for promoter recognition). Rifampin inhibits bacterial RNA polymerase. Reference: Harper's — Transcription.

questions.questionText: The central dogma of molecular biology states:
questions.optionB: DNA → RNA → Protein (genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to protein)
questions.explanation: Central dogma (Francis Crick, 1958): DNA → (transcription) → RNA → (translation) → Protein. Exceptions: reverse transcriptase (RNA → DNA, in retroviruses like HIV), RNA replication (RNA → RNA, in RNA viruses), prions (protein → protein misfolding). DNA replication: DNA → DNA. Translation occurs on ribosomes (70S in prokaryotes = 30S + 50S; 80S in eukaryotes = 40S + 60S). Reference: Harper's — Central Dogma.

questions.questionText: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) requires all EXCEPT:
questions.optionC: Primers (two), dNTPs, and Taq DNA polymerase
questions.explanation: PCR components: (1) Template DNA, (2) Two primers (flanking the target), (3) dNTPs (dATP, dGTP, dCTP, dTTP), (4) Taq DNA polymerase (thermostable, from Thermus aquaticus), (5) MgCl2 (cofactor), (6) Buffer. Thermal cycler performs cycles: denaturation (~94°C), annealing (~55-65°C), extension (~72°C). ~30 cycles → ~10^9 amplification. RNA polymerase is NOT used in standard PCR. RT-PCR: uses reverse transcriptase first (RNA → cDNA), then PCR. Reference: Harper's — PCR.

questions.questionText: Post-transcriptional modification of mRNA in eukaryotes includes:
questions.optionB: 5' capping (7-methylguanosine), 3' polyadenylation (poly-A tail), and splicing (intron removal)
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic mRNA processing: (1) 5' cap: 7-methylguanosine (m7G) added co-transcriptionally → protects from exonuclease, aids ribosome binding. (2) 3' poly-A tail: 100-250 adenines added by poly-A polymerase → mRNA stability, nuclear export. (3) Splicing: removal of introns (non-coding) by spliceosome (snRNPs — U1, U2, U4, U5, U6). Alternative splicing → one gene → multiple proteins. Prokaryotic mRNA: NO cap, NO poly-A, NO introns (polycistronic). Reference: Harper's — mRNA Processing.

questions.questionText: Restriction enzymes are used in recombinant DNA technology to:
questions.optionB: Cut DNA at specific palindromic recognition sequences
questions.explanation: Restriction endonucleases: cut dsDNA at specific palindromic sequences (4-8 bp recognition sites). Type II are most useful (cut within recognition site). Examples: EcoRI (GAATTC — produces sticky/cohesive ends), HindIII (AAGCTT), BamHI (GGATCC). Some produce blunt ends (e.g., HaeIII). Used for: gene cloning, DNA mapping, RFLP analysis, genetic fingerprinting. DNA ligase joins the cut fragments (recombinant molecules). Named after bacteria of origin. Reference: Harper's — Restriction Enzymes.

Blotting techniques: Southern blot: DNA detection (invented by Edwin Southern). Steps: DNA digested by restriction enzymes → gel electrophoresis → transfer to nitrocellulose/nylon membrane → hybridize with labeled probe → autoradiography. Northern blot: RNA detection. Western blot: protein detection (using antibodies — e.g., confirmatory test for HIV). Eastern blot: post-translational modifications (glycosylation, lipids). Southwestern: protein-DNA interactions. Reference: Harper's — Blotting.

questions.optionB: Non-standard base pairing at the 3rd position of the codon (1st position of anticodon) → fewer tRNAs needed
questions.explanation: Wobble hypothesis (Crick): the 3rd base of the codon (5' base of anticodon) can form non-standard (wobble) base pairs. Example: inosine (I) in anticodon can pair with U, C, or A. This allows a single tRNA to recognize multiple codons coding for the same amino acid → 61 sense codons can be read by ~45 tRNAs. This explains the degeneracy of the genetic code (multiple codons for same amino acid). Reference: Harper's — Wobble.

questions.questionText: The genetic code is degenerate, which means:
questions.optionA: One codon codes for multiple amino acids
questions.optionB: Multiple codons can code for the same amino acid
questions.explanation: Genetic code properties: (1) Degenerate/redundant: multiple codons for same amino acid (64 codons for 20 AAs — 61 sense + 3 stop). (2) Non-overlapping: codons read sequentially without overlap. (3) Non-ambiguous: each codon specifies only ONE amino acid. (4) Universal (with minor exceptions — mitochondria, some organisms). (5) Commaless: no gaps between codons. Start codon: AUG (methionine). Stop codons: UAA (ochre), UAG (amber), UGA (opal). Trp and Met have single codons. Reference: Harper's — Genetic Code.

questions.optionB: Precise genome editing (targeted gene modification using guide RNA)
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9: revolutionary genome editing tool. Guide RNA (sgRNA) directs Cas9 endonuclease to a specific DNA sequence (complementary) → Cas9 creates double-strand break → repaired by NHEJ (gene disruption/knockout) or HDR (precise gene correction/insertion). Applications: gene therapy (sickle cell — approved treatment), disease modeling, agriculture, drug development. Discovered from bacterial adaptive immune system against phages. Nobel Prize 2020 (Charpentier and Doudna). Reference: Harper's — CRISPR.

questions.optionB: DNA from RNA template (RNA-dependent DNA polymerase)
questions.explanation: Reverse transcriptase (RT): RNA-dependent DNA polymerase. Found in retroviruses (HIV, HTLV). Synthesizes cDNA (complementary DNA) from RNA template. Also has RNase H activity (degrades RNA in RNA-DNA hybrid). Used in lab: cDNA library construction, RT-PCR (detect RNA viruses, measure gene expression). HIV RT: lacks proofreading (error-prone → high mutation rate → drug resistance). Anti-HIV drugs: NRTIs (zidovudine) and NNRTIs (efavirenz) target RT. Reference: Harper's — Reverse Transcriptase.

questions.questionText: Lac operon is an example of:
questions.explanation: Lac operon (E. coli, Jacob-Monod model): inducible operon — normally repressed (lac repressor binds operator → blocks transcription). When lactose present (as allolactose) → binds repressor → conformational change → repressor falls off → transcription proceeds. Maximal expression requires: allolactose (removes repressor) + cAMP-CAP (positive regulator, when glucose is LOW — catabolite repression). Trp operon: repressible (tryptophan is co-repressor). Reference: Harper's — Lac Operon.

questions.questionText: DNA damage repair by nucleotide excision repair (NER) is defective in:
questions.explanation: Xeroderma pigmentosum: AR, defective nucleotide excision repair (NER) → cannot repair UV-induced thymine dimers (pyrimidine dimers). Extreme UV sensitivity, photosensitivity, multiple skin cancers (BCC, SCC, melanoma) at young age, neurological degeneration. 7 complementation groups (XPA-XPG). NER repairs bulky lesions (UV dimers, chemical adducts). Other repair defects: HNPCC/Lynch (mismatch repair — MSH2, MLH1), Bloom syndrome (DNA helicase), Fanconi anemia (DNA crosslink repair). Reference: Harper's — XP.

questions.questionText: Translation initiation in eukaryotes begins with which amino acid?
questions.optionB: Methionine (AUG start codon, recognized by initiator tRNA-Met)
questions.explanation: Translation initiation: AUG = start codon → methionine (in eukaryotes). In prokaryotes: N-formylmethionine (fMet). Eukaryotic translation: cap-dependent scanning (40S subunit + eIF factors bind 5' cap → scan for AUG → 60S joins → 80S ribosome). Prokaryotic: Shine-Dalgarno sequence on mRNA binds 16S rRNA → positions AUG. Antibiotics targeting translation: chloramphenicol (50S, peptidyl transferase), tetracycline (30S, tRNA binding), erythromycin (50S, translocation). Reference: Harper's — Translation.

questions.optionB: Gain-of-function mutations (point mutations, amplification, translocation → constitutive activation)
questions.explanation: Proto-oncogenes → oncogenes: gain-of-function (dominant — one allele sufficient). Mechanisms: (1) Point mutation (RAS — most commonly mutated oncogene, constitutively active GTPase). (2) Gene amplification (HER2/ERBB2 in breast cancer, N-MYC in neuroblastoma). (3) Chromosomal translocation (BCR-ABL t(9;22) in CML — Philadelphia chromosome; c-MYC t(8;14) in Burkitt lymphoma). Tumor suppressors: loss-of-function (both alleles — two-hit hypothesis, Knudson). Reference: Harper's — Oncogenes.

questions.questionText: Which DNA repair pathway primarily removes bulky thymine dimers induced by UV radiation?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Nucleotide excision repair removes bulky DNA adducts like pyrimidine dimers; defects cause xeroderma pigmentosum with photosensitivity and skin cancer risk.

questions.questionText: Southern blot is used to detect:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Southern blot detects specific DNA sequences. Northern detects RNA, Western detects proteins.

questions.questionText: In prokaryotes, the lac operon is induced in the presence of lactose when the repressor:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Allolactose (inducer) binds lac repressor and reduces operator binding, permitting transcription. CAP-cAMP further enhances expression when glucose is low.
